caveats: a: O*NET gives task incidence rather than task-time weights.
a: Advanced automated fabs and small specialty-device plants have very different labor mixes.
a: Metrology automation and conventional statistical process control should not be attributed wholly to generative AI.

rho: Older tools may lack sensors, interfaces, or consistent data history.

e: GlobalFoundries and its ecosystem are much larger and broader than an LMM target.

s5: Security or supply-chain importance may favor strategic or government-supported outcomes.

q: Qualification creates stickiness but also high costs for the supplier.

d5: Nominal industry sales include price and product-mix effects.
d5: Public investment can create excess capacity as well as demand.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include industrial, automotive, aerospace and defense, medical, communications, data-center, IoT, and research organizations. Revenue is earned from wafers, dice, devices, engineering runs, prototypes, qualification, masks and nonrecurring engineering, then recurring production releases over a customer's product life cycle.
lens.excluded_activities: Fabless chip design without manufacturing; captive semiconductor plants; pure equipment, materials, packaging, and test providers classified elsewhere; commodity memory and leading-edge logic platforms requiring global scale; undifferentiated solar-cell capacity; and large integrated device manufacturers outside the LMM band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market specialty semiconductor and related-device manufacturers, including mature-node and trusted foundries, analog, power, sensor, optoelectronic, photonic, discrete-device, and custom-wafer businesses with high-mix production, customer-funded development, qualification, and repeat manufacturing programs.

a.bridge: O*NET's semiconductor-technician tasks and software are translated into total labor-hour exposure, while NIST evidence on defect inspection, classification, metrology, and process control supports the technical scope for machine-assisted analysis. The task list directly represents fab work but does not provide time shares or measured generative-AI savings.
caveats: O*NET gives task incidence rather than task-time weights.
Advanced automated fabs and small specialty-device plants have very different labor mixes.
Metrology automation and conventional statistical process control should not be attributed wholly to generative AI.
a.change_evidence: Representative time-and-motion and deployment data for specialty fabs measuring labor, engineering cycle time, yield, scrap, escapes, uptime, recipe development, documentation, and customer-support changes attributable to AI.
a.rationale: AI can assist recipe and process-window analysis, yield learning, defect classification, metrology review, equipment anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, scheduling, work instructions, quality records, quoting, design enablement, and customer support. Cleanroom material movement, chamber loading, hazardous chemistry, maintenance, calibration, physical inspection, and high-consequence process decisions remain embodied or expert-supervised.

d5.bridge: SIA's observed 2024 sales and 2025 forecast, cited application drivers, and projected U.S. capacity expansion, together with CHIPS for America's funding and end-use scope, are translated into five-year real demand for the LMM specialty subset. Aggregate global growth may concentrate in memory and leading-edge logic, while domestic incentives may disproportionately favor large facilities.
caveats: Global semiconductor sales are not an LMM specialty forecast.
Nominal industry sales include price and product-mix effects.
Public investment can create excess capacity as well as demand.
d5.change_evidence: Independent 2026-2031 volume, pricing, capacity, and utilization forecasts for U.S. specialty foundry, analog, power, sensor, photonic, discrete, and trusted-device segments, separated from memory and leading-edge logic.
d5.rationale: AI infrastructure, data centers, power management, sensing, photonics, communications, vehicles, industrial automation, defense, clean energy, and domestic supply-chain policy support demand. Specialty mature-node devices can benefit even when they are not the accelerator itself. Offsets include severe semiconductor cycles, inventory corrections, export controls, pricing pressure, technology migration, customer concentration, and large capacity additions.

e.bridge: GlobalFoundries' partner ecosystem documents customer movement from design handoff through tapeout, multi-project wafers, qualification, ramp, sustained manufacturing, and lifecycle support. That large-platform evidence is mapped to the share of LMM semiconductor firms offering service-like custom development and repeat programs; it may overstate breadth and formal support at small plants.
caveats: Repeat wafer orders are not subscription revenue.
Foundry service models may be underrepresented among the frozen 214-firm universe.
GlobalFoundries and its ecosystem are much larger and broader than an LMM target.
e.change_evidence: Establishment-level revenue by custom engineering, masks and NRE, prototyping, qualification, wafer services, repeat production, catalog products, and commodity output, plus customer retention and program duration.
e.rationale: Specialty foundries and custom analog, power, sensor, photonic, and trusted-device manufacturers can become embedded through process development, masks, qualification, technical support, repeat wafer releases, and long product lives. Commodity discrete devices, memory, standard catalog parts, and undifferentiated solar cells remain primarily product transactions, keeping eligibility well below the whole industry.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $17,865.5M / sectoral output (T30) $55,464.5M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $611,842 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334413); class <5: 252 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,147,599 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $290,572 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,670,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $676,044 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,133,476 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,046,596 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 130 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,645,285 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,961,386 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $65,373,700 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,552,621 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 72 firms, avg revenue/firm $836,504,542 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $211,802,950 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 214

caveats: The factor isolates AI-related obsolescence from ordinary semiconductor cycles.
Individual products and process nodes can become obsolete rapidly.
Solar-cell and commodity-device economics differ materially from specialty foundries.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that AI-driven architecture, integration, material substitution, or customer insourcing broadly eliminates demand for external specialty-device fabrication rather than shifting demand among device types and nodes.
o.rationale: AI systems and nearly every digital or electrified end market require physical semiconductors; AI therefore complements rather than eliminates device manufacturing. Particular nodes, materials, device architectures, solar technologies, or catalog parts can become obsolete, and customers can migrate to integrated or imported alternatives, but software cannot remove the physical chip function over five years.

q.bridge: GlobalFoundries' documented qualification, validated technologies, technical engagement, prototyping-to-volume path, and lifecycle continuity are mapped to the fraction of AI-created value retained by LMM specialty manufacturers. The platform's scale and ecosystem may overstate bargaining power, while small trusted or single-source programs can create unusually strong retention.
caveats: Capture is much lower for standard catalog devices than custom or trusted programs.
Large OEMs can have substantial purchasing leverage.
Qualification creates stickiness but also high costs for the supplier.
q.change_evidence: Program-level pricing, NRE terms, yield-sharing, annual repricing, customer concentration, qualification time, dual-source status, retention, and margin changes after AI-enabled productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Qualified processes, masks, device redesign costs, long validation cycles, trusted sourcing, specialized know-how, and yield risk make switching expensive. Firms can retain value from faster development, improved yield, fewer escapes, higher uptime, and reliable delivery, although sophisticated customers, dual sourcing, annual price negotiations, foundry competition, and transparent catalog pricing force pass-through.

caveats: No representative adoption study for the LMM specialty subset was found.
Closed-loop process changes face a higher validation threshold than decision support.
Older tools may lack sensors, interfaces, or consistent data history.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-site evidence on AI deployment cost, adoption, validation time, data readiness, uptime, yield, cycle time, engineering hours, and payback across mature-node foundries and specialty device plants.
rho.rationale: Fabs generate dense digital equipment, inspection, metrology, yield, and manufacturing-execution data, making anomaly detection, vision, analytics, scheduling, and knowledge tools technically accessible. Realization is limited by sparse failure examples, proprietary process data, legacy tools, integration cost, cybersecurity and export controls, qualification, strict change control, false-positive risk, and scarce process engineers at LMM firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup's 2025 cross-industry survey of owner age and five-year disposition intentions is mapped to actionable acquisition availability among qualifying specialty semiconductor manufacturers. It may overstate availability because export controls, environmental liabilities, specialized facilities, customer qualification, and reinvestment narrow the buyer pool.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not observed semiconductor transactions.
A plant can be available yet uneconomic after required reinvestment.
Security or supply-chain importance may favor strategic or government-supported outcomes.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year transaction, closure, succession, and failed-sale data for independent U.S. specialty fabs and related-device manufacturers, including owner age, facility condition, qualifications, permits, capex backlog, and buyer type.
s5.rationale: The frozen 214-firm LMM universe is sizable, but a financeable seller needs defensible process IP, maintained cleanroom and fab equipment, environmental compliance, qualified staff, recurring programs, transferable customer approvals, and a manageable technology roadmap. Strategic buyers and public funding can also reduce the pool available to a financial acquirer.

principal_driver: AI expands semiconductor demand while the same data-rich fab environment creates large opportunities in yield, inspection, process control, uptime, and engineering productivity.
principal_weakness: Extreme capital intensity and rapid technology change can overwhelm labor savings when a plant lacks differentiated, qualified, recurring customer programs.
